title: Collections Framework Internals
subtitle: Buckets, collisions, treeification, and the real cost of resize
goal: Reason about java.util.HashMap the way the JDK source does — predict bucket placement, collision chains, treeification, and rehash cost — and pick HashMap vs TreeMap vs LinkedHashMap by their internal invariants instead of vibes.

blocks.heading: The bucket array is the whole game
blocks.html:
A HashMap is a Node<K,V>[] table — an array of buckets. Every put computes which slot a key lands in. With a table of length n (always a power of two), the index is (n - 1) & hash. That bitwise AND is a cheap modulo: because n is a power of two, (n-1) is a mask of the low bits, so the index is just the bottom log2(n) bits of hash.

That is also the trap. If you only used the raw hashCode(), two keys whose low bits collide would always share a bucket even if their high bits differ wildly. The JDK fixes this with a spread step.

blocks.caption: HashMap.hash — the spread function (verbatim from the JDK)
blocks.explain: XOR-ing the high half into the low half means a key whose entropy lives in its top bits still influences the bucket choice. null keys hash to 0 and always live in bucket 0. This single line is why HashMap tolerates mediocre hashCode() implementations gracefully — it is the difference between O(1) and a degenerate chain.

blocks.heading: Collisions: linked list first, red-black tree later
blocks.html:
When two keys map to the same bucket, the bucket becomes a chain of Nodes (a singly-linked list). Lookup walks the chain, comparing with hash first (cheap int compare) and only then equals(). A short chain is fine. A long chain is O(k).

To bound worst case, once a single bucket holds TREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 8 nodes and the table is at least MIN_TREEIFY_CAPACITY = 64, that bucket converts from a linked list into a red-black tree, turning per-bucket lookup from O(k) into O(log k). If the table is smaller than 64, HashMap resizes instead of treeifying — more buckets usually scatters the colliding keys. When a tree shrinks back to UNTREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 6 nodes it reverts to a list.

Treeification needs both conditions: chain length ≥ 8 and table capacity ≥ 64. People memorize "8" and forget the 64 — a tiny map with 8 colliding keys will resize, not treeify. Also: treeification falls back to insertion-order if your keys are not Comparable, so a custom key with a broken or absent compareTo still works, just slower.

blocks.heading: The hashCode / equals contract
blocks.html:
Three rules, and breaking any one corrupts a HashMap silently:

  1. Consistency: equal objects (a.equals(b)) MUST return equal hash codes. Violate this and you can put(key) then fail to get(key) with an equal key — they land in different buckets.
  2. Stability: an object's hashCode must not change while it is a key (don't mutate fields used by equals/hashCode after insertion).
  3. equals symmetry/transitivity/reflexivity must hold, or membership tests become nondeterministic.

Unequal objects may share a hashCode (that is just a collision, and is legal) — but equal objects sharing a hashCode is mandatory.

blocks.heading: Load factor 0.75 and the cost of resize
blocks.html:
HashMap grows when size > threshold, where threshold = capacity × loadFactor and the default load factor is 0.75. So a default-capacity-16 map resizes once it would hold its 13th entry (16 × 0.75 = 12, exceed it → grow). Resize doubles capacity and rehashes every existing entry into the new, larger table — an O(n) burst.

0.75 is the time/space compromise: lower load factor = fewer collisions but more wasted slots; higher = denser but longer chains. Because each resize doubles, the amortized cost of n inserts stays O(n), but any single insert can trigger a full O(n) rehash. In a latency-sensitive hot path you pre-size: new HashMap<>(expectedSize / 0.75 + 1) so you never pay an unplanned rehash mid-flight.

blocks.caption: Capacity is rounded UP to the next power of two
blocks.explain: Whatever initial capacity you request, HashMap rounds it up to a power of two so the (n-1)&hash trick stays valid. Requesting 1000 actually allocates 1024 buckets. This is why pre-sizing with expected/0.75 matters: it controls which power-of-two band you land in and how soon the first resize fires.

blocks.heading: HashMap vs TreeMap vs LinkedHashMap
blocks.html:
MapBacking structureIteration orderget/putOrdering / nulls
HashMapbucket array + chains/treesunspecified (depends on hash & capacity, can change on resize)O(1) averageno order; one null key allowed
LinkedHashMapHashMap + doubly-linked node listinsertion order, or access order if accessOrder=trueO(1) averagenull key allowed; tiny per-node memory overhead
TreeMapred-black treesorted by key (natural or Comparator)O(log n)keys must be Comparable / have Comparator; no null key (NPE)

Reach for TreeMap when you need range queries (floorKey, ceilingKey, subMap). Reach for LinkedHashMap with accessOrder=true + an overridden removeEldestEntry to build an LRU cache in ~5 lines. Reach for HashMap by default for raw throughput when order is irrelevant.

LinkedHashMap with accessOrder=true moves an entry to the tail on every get and put. Override protected boolean removeEldestEntry(Map.Entry) to return size() > capacity and you have a self-evicting LRU. The head is the least-recently-used; the tail is the most-recently-used.

blocks.question: You insert 8 keys that all collide in the same bucket of a brand-new default HashMap (capacity 16). What happens to that bucket?
options: It immediately converts to a red-black tree (8 ≥ TREEIFY_THRESHOLD).
Nothing special — it stays a linked list because capacity (16) < MIN_TREEIFY_CAPACITY (64), so HashMap resizes instead.
The map throws because chains cannot exceed 8.
It converts to a tree only if the keys are not Comparable.
blocks.explain: Treeification requires chain length ≥ 8 AND table capacity ≥ 64. At capacity 16 the map resizes (grows the table) rather than treeify, on the bet that more buckets will scatter the colliding keys.

blocks.question: Which call sequence makes a LinkedHashMap behave as an LRU cache that evicts the least-recently-used entry past a size cap?
options: new LinkedHashMap<>(cap) and call sort() before eviction
new LinkedHashMap<>(cap, 0.75f, true) and override removeEldestEntry to return size() > cap
new TreeMap<>() with a timestamp Comparator
new HashMap<>(cap, 0.75f) and manually track order in a separate list
blocks.explain: The third constructor arg accessOrder=true makes get/put reorder to most-recently-used at the tail; removeEldestEntry lets the map drop the head (LRU) automatically once size exceeds the cap.

blocks.title: Compute a bucket index by hand
blocks.instructions: Print the HashMap bucket index for key 17 in a table of length 16, using spread(h)=h^(h>>>16) then (n-1)&spread. For an Integer, hashCode() == the int value.
blocks.passMsg: Correct — 17 lands in bucket 1 (17 & 15 = 1; spread leaves small ints unchanged).
blocks.failMsg: Bucket index is (n-1) & (h ^ (h>>>16)). For h=17, n=16 that is 15 & 17 = 1.

problems.title: Bucket collision tracker with treeify prediction
problems.instructions:
Model exactly how HashMap places keys into buckets. Read an integer table capacity n (always a power of two, 1 ≤ n ≤ 2^20) on the first line, then an integer k (number of keys) on the second line, then k integer keys (one per line).

For each key compute its bucket as (n - 1) & (h ^ (h >>> 16)) where h is the key's Integer.hashCode() (i.e. the int value itself). Count how many keys fall in each bucket.

Output, in ascending bucket-index order, one line per non-empty bucket:
<bucketIndex> <count> <TREEIFY|list>
Print TREEIFY when that bucket would convert to a red-black tree, i.e. count >= 8 AND n >= 64; otherwise print list. After the per-bucket lines, print a final summary line max <maxCount> giving the largest single-bucket count.

tests.name: hidden: capacity 64, one bucket reaches 8 -> TREEIFY
hidden: capacity 64, only 7 in a bucket -> still list

problems.hint: Integer.hashCode() is the value itself; spread(h)=h^(h>>>16); index=(n-1)&spread. TREEIFY needs count>=8 AND n>=64. Use a TreeMap<Integer,Integer> so buckets print in ascending order automatically.

problems.title: LRU cache via LinkedHashMap accessOrder
problems.instructions:
Build an LRU cache backed by LinkedHashMap in access-order mode. First line: capacity C (1 ≤ C ≤ 100000). Second line: number of operations Q. Then Q operations, one per line:

  • PUT key value — insert/update; both ints.
  • GET key — print the value, or -1 if absent. A hit counts as a use (moves the key to most-recently-used).

When inserting past capacity, evict the least-recently-used entry. After all operations, print a final line keys <list> = the keys currently in the cache from least- to most-recently-used, space-separated (print keys with nothing after it if empty). Only GET operations produce output before that line.
problems.stdin:
2
6
PUT 1 10
PUT 2 20
GET 1
PUT 3 30
GET 2
GET 3

tests.name: visible: classic LRU eviction
tests.stdin:
2
6
PUT 1 10
PUT 2 20
GET 1
PUT 3 30
GET 2
GET 3

tests.name: hidden: update refreshes recency, no eviction

problems.hint: new LinkedHashMap<>(initialCap, 0.75f, true) gives access order; override removeEldestEntry to return size()>cap. keySet() iterates LRU-first to MRU-last, exactly the order you must print.

problems.title: Open-addressing hash map: linear-probe counter
problems.instructions:
Implement an open-addressing hash table with linear probing (no chaining) and report probe costs — the technique behind cache-friendly HFT maps. Fixed table length m on line 1 (power of two, 2 ≤ m ≤ 2^20). Line 2: number of inserts k (guaranteed k < m, so the table never fills). Then k integer keys.

Slot for a key = (m - 1) & (h ^ (h >>> 16)) with h = Integer.hashCode(key). On collision, probe linearly forward with wraparound: try slot, slot+1, slot+2, … mod m until an empty slot is found. Probe count for one insert = number of slots examined, counting the final empty slot (so a key that lands in its home slot directly costs 1). Duplicate keys do not occur.

Output one line per insert in input order: the probe count for that key. Then a final line total <sum> with the sum of all probe counts.

tests.name: visible: three keys collide at slot 0, then linear probe
hidden: no collisions, all home slots
hidden: wraparound probe at end of table

problems.hint: Home slot = (m-1) & spread(h). Walk idx=(idx+1)&mask while occupied, counting every slot you look at including the empty one you settle into. A direct hit costs exactly 1.

problems.title: Resize cost counter: rehash threshold crossings
problems.instructions:
Track HashMap growth. Inputs: line 1 = initial requested capacity reqCap (1 ≤ reqCap ≤ 2^20); line 2 = number of distinct keys inserted k (1 ≤ k ≤ 5,000,000). Assume the default load factor 0.75 and that all k inserts are distinct keys (so size grows by 1 each insert).

HashMap rounds the initial capacity up to the next power of two (tableSizeFor); the first threshold = capacity × 0.75 (floored). A resize fires when, after an insert, size > threshold; each resize doubles capacity and recomputes the threshold. Simulate inserting keys 1..k.

Output one line per resize, in order:
resize <oldCap> -> <newCap> at size <size>
(size = the live entry count that triggered the resize). Then a final line final cap <capacity> rehashes <count> giving the ending table capacity and total number of resizes.

tests.name: visible: default 16, 13th insert triggers first resize
tests.expectStdout:
resize 16 -> 32 at size 13
final cap 32 rehashes 1

tests.name: hidden: no resize when under threshold
hidden: requested 33 rounds to 64, two resizes by 100
tests.expectStdout:
resize 64 -> 128 at size 49
resize 128 -> 256 at size 97
final cap 256 rehashes 2

tests.name: stress: 5,000,000 inserts amortized O(n)
tests.expectStdout:
resize 16 -> 32 at size 13
resize 32 -> 64 at size 25
resize 64 -> 128 at size 49
resize 128 -> 256 at size 97
resize 256 -> 512 at size 193
resize 512 -> 1024 at size 385
resize 1024 -> 2048 at size 769
resize 2048 -> 4096 at size 1537
resize 4096 -> 8192 at size 3073
resize 8192 -> 16384 at size 6145
resize 16384 -> 32768 at size 12289
resize 32768 -> 65536 at size 24577
resize 65536 -> 131072 at size 49153
resize 131072 -> 262144 at size 98305
resize 262144 -> 524288 at size 196609
resize 524288 -> 1048576 at size 393217
resize 1048576 -> 2097152 at size 786433
resize 2097152 -> 4194304 at size 1572865
resize 4194304 -> 8388608 at size 3145729
final cap 8388608 rehashes 19

problems.hint: tableSizeFor rounds reqCap up to a power of two. threshold = floor(cap*0.75). Resize when size>threshold AFTER the insert, then double cap. The classic 16-map first grows on the 13th insert.
